title: Charmed Life - Chapter 11 (Fragment 3)
text:
“Oh dear!” said Janet. “They know.”

“Give them to me,” said Cat. “Quick. Someone will hear.”

Janet poured the earrings into Cat’s palm. The voices stopped at once. “I can’t get used to all this magic,” said Janet. “Cat, what am I to do? How can I pay that horrible man?”

“There must be something we can sell,” said Cat. “There’s a junk shop in the village. Come on. We must get to lunch.”

They hurried up to the playroom, to find that Mary had already put plates of stew and dumplings in their places.

“Oh, look,” said Janet, who needed to relieve her feelings somehow. “Nourishing fattening lunch. How nice!”

Mary glared at both of them and left the room without speaking. Julia’s look was quite as unpleasant. As Janet sat down in front of her stew, Julia pulled her handkerchief out of her sleeve, already knotted, and laid it in her lap. Janet put her fork into a dumpling. It stuck there. The dumpling was a white pebble, swimming with two others in a plateful of mud.

Janet carefully laid down her fork, with the pebble impaled on it, and put her knife neatly across the mud. She was trying to control herself but, for a moment, she looked like Gwendolen at her most furious. “I was quite hungry,” she said.

Julia smiled. “What a pity,” she said cosily. “And you’ve got no witchcraft to defend yourself with, have you?” She tied another smaller knot at the end of her handkerchief. “You’ve got all sorts of things in your hair, Gwendolen,” she said as she pulled it tight. The twigs sticking in Janet’s hair writhed and began dropping on the table and over her skirt. Each one was a large, stripy caterpillar.

Janet was no more bothered by wriggly things than Gwendolen. She picked the caterpillars off and put them in a heap in front of Julia. “I’ve a good mind to shout for your father,” she said.

“Oh, no, don’t be a telltale,” said Roger. “Let her be, Julia.”

“Certainly not,” said Julia. “She’s not getting any lunch.”

After the meeting with Mr. Baslam, Cat was not really very hungry. “Here,” he said, and changed his plate of stew with Janet’s mud. Janet started to protest. But, as soon as the plate of mud was in front of Cat, it was steaming stew again. And the looping heap of caterpillars was simply a pile of twigs.

Julia turned to Cat, not at all pleased. “Don’t you interfere. You annoy me. She treats you like a slave and all you do is stick up for her.”

“But I only changed the plates!” Cat said, puzzled. “Why—?”

“It could have been Michael,” Roger suggested.

Julia glowered at him too. “Was it you?” Roger blandly shook his head. Julia looked at him uncertainly. “If I have to go without marmalade again,” she said at length, “Gwendolen’s going to know about it. And I hope the stew chokes you.”

Cat found it hard to concentrate on lessons that afternoon. He had to watch Janet like a hawk. Janet had decided that the only safe thing was to be totally stupid—she thought Gwendolen must have been pretty stupid anyway—and Cat knew she was overdoing it. Even Gwendolen had known the twice-times table. Cat was worried too, in case Julia started knotting that handkerchief of hers when Mr. Saunders’ back was turned. Luckily, Julia did not quite dare. But Cat’s main worry was how to find twenty pounds by next Wednesday. He could hardly bear to think of what might happen if he did not. The very least thing, he knew, would be Janet confessing she was not Gwendolen. He thought of Chrestomanci giving him that scathing stare and saying, “ You went with Gwendolen to buy dragons’ blood, Eric? But you knew it was illegal. And you tried to cover up by making Janet pretend to be Gwendolen? You show touching concern, Eric.” The mere idea made Cat shrivel up inside. But he had nothing
to sell except a pair of earrings that shouted that they belonged to someone else. If he wrote to the Mayor of Wolvercote and asked if he could have twenty pounds out of the Fund, the Mayor would only write to Chrestomanci to ask why Cat wanted it. And then Chrestomanci would stare scathingly and say, “ You went with Gwendolen to buy dragons’ blood, Eric?” It was hopeless.

vocabulary.definition: To make someone a little angry or irritated.
vocabulary.example: “Don’t you interfere. You annoy me.”

vocabulary.definition: To work together with someone to achieve something.
vocabulary.example: (Relevant advanced verb related to narrative collaboration, p.o.v. in context.)

vocabulary.definition: To admit that something is true, often something bad or embarrassing.
vocabulary.example: The very least thing, he knew, would be Janet confessing she was not Gwendolen.

vocabulary.definition: Adjectives that provide detail about the characteristics of a noun.
vocabulary.example: Examples in the text: nourishing, fattening, furious, unpleasant.

vocabulary.definition: Small pieces of dough cooked by boiling, steaming, or frying, often served with stew.
vocabulary.example: Janet put her fork into a dumpling.

vocabulary.definition: Causing weight gain, usually due to high fat or sugar content.
vocabulary.example: “Nourishing fattening lunch.”

vocabulary.definition: To look at someone angrily or fiercely.
vocabulary.example: Mary glared at both of them and left the room without speaking.

vocabulary.definition: A large pile or mound of something.
vocabulary.example: She picked the caterpillars off and put them in a heap in front of Julia.

vocabulary.definition: Weakened or damaged, especially referring to abilities or functions.
Easily annoyed by delays or waiting.
vocabulary.example: (Inferred in urgency: “Quick. Someone will hear.”)

vocabulary.definition: To get involved in a situation where you are not wanted or needed, especially to prevent something from succeeding.
vocabulary.example: Julia turned to Cat, not at all pleased. “Don’t you interfere.”

vocabulary.definition: To work dough or clay by pressing and folding it.
vocabulary.example: (Not directly from text, included as advanced verb related to description of actions.)

vocabulary.definition: A pile arranged or appearing to loop or twist unusually.
vocabulary.example: And the looping heap of caterpillars was simply a pile of twigs.

vocabulary.definition: A combination of words that functions as a single unit of meaning.
vocabulary.example: Examples: scathing stare, telltale sign.

vocabulary.definition: Verbs commonly used to tell a story or describe actions in narrative form.
vocabulary.example: Examples extracted like hurried, poured, glared.

vocabulary.definition: Providing the substances necessary for growth, health, and good condition.
vocabulary.example: “Nourishing fattening lunch.”

vocabulary.definition: To act as if something is true when it is not.
vocabulary.example: ...making Janet pretend to be Gwendolen?

vocabulary.definition: To confuse or perplex someone by being difficult to understand.
vocabulary.example: Cat said, puzzled. “Why—?”

vocabulary.definition: To reduce or remove pain, stress or an unpleasant feeling.
vocabulary.example: Janet needed to relieve her feelings somehow.

vocabulary.definition: Severely critical or scornful.
vocabulary.example: The mere idea made Cat shrivel up inside. But he had nothing to sell except a pair of earrings that shouted that they belonged to someone else. If he wrote to the Mayor of Wolvercote and asked if he could have twenty pounds out of the Fund, the Mayor would only write to Chrestomanci to ask why Cat wanted it. And then Chrestomanci would stare scathingly and say, “ You went with Gwendolen to buy dragons’ blood, Eric?” It was hopeless.

vocabulary.definition: A very critical and harsh look.
vocabulary.example: ...Chrestomanci giving him that scathing stare and saying...

vocabulary.definition: Feeling or expressing contempt or disdain.
vocabulary.example: (Synonymous tone highlighted in 'scathing' context.)

vocabulary.definition: Emitting steam; very hot.
vocabulary.example: But, as soon as the plate of mud was in front of Cat, it was steaming stew again.

vocabulary.definition: A dish of meat and vegetables cooked slowly in liquid.
vocabulary.example: Janet sat down in front of her stew.

vocabulary.definition: Indicating or revealing something that is usually secret.
vocabulary.example: “Oh, no, don’t be a telltale,” said Roger.

vocabulary.definition: Requiring immediate action or attention.
vocabulary.example: “Quick. Someone will hear.”

vocabulary.definition: To twist or move your body without stopping due to pain, discomfort, or struggle.
vocabulary.example: The twigs sticking in Janet’s hair writhed and began dropping on the table.
